prior_content_md:
## ACTION REQUIRED — Confirm No Direct "Company" Obligations Exist

FACT: "The entity responsible for ensuring compliance with this Regulation should be the company, defined as the shipowner or any other organisation or person, such as the manager or the bareboat charterer, that has assumed responsibility for the operation of the ship from the shipowner." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Recital 20 and Article 3(13), European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Legal counsel must confirm whether the workspace, in any of its transaction types involving ocean carriage, meets this defined term. If it does, monitoring plan submission, annual FuelEU reporting, and penalty exposure attach directly. This determination cannot be made from operational description alone.

**Action:** Brief legal counsel now. Resolution needed before the 31 January 2026 FuelEU report deadline.

---

## COST ALERT — FuelEU Penalty Exposure Is Passing Through Freight Rates from 2026

FACT: The FuelEU penalty for GHG intensity non-compliance is EUR 2,400 per tonne of VLSFO energy equivalent (where 1 metric tonne VLSFO = 41,000 MJ). FACT: For consecutive reporting periods of non-compliance, the penalty amount is "multiplied by 1 + (n-1)/10, where n is the number of consecutive reporting periods for which the company is subject to a FuelEU penalty for that ship." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Article 23(2) and Annex IV Part B, European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

FACT: The penalty for OPS non-compliance is "EUR 1,5 by the established total electrical power demand of the ship at berth and by the total number of hours rounded up to the nearest whole hour, spent at berth by the ship in non-compliance." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Article 23(5), European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

*Operational implication:* Carriers facing penalty exposure will seek to recover costs through fuel surcharges or freight rate increases. The first penalties are due by 30 June 2026, based on the 2025 reporting period. For workspaces procuring ocean freight on transatlantic and transpacific trade lanes calling at EU ports, a cost increase vector is now active.

**Action:** Request from each carrier partner a written statement of their FuelEU compliance status for the 2025 reporting year, and confirm whether any FuelEU-related surcharge has been or will be applied to rates from Q2 2026 onward. Add this to rate review and contract renewal processes immediately.

---

## WINDOW CLOSING — Carrier Contract Clause Review Before June 2026 Penalty Deadline

FACT: The regulation expressly enables contractual liability transfer: "the entity responsible for purchasing the fuel or for taking operational decisions that affect the GHG intensity of the energy used by the ship could, through contractual agreements with the company, in the event of compliance deficit, be put under the obligation to reimburse or otherwise compensate the company with respect to the cost of the FuelEU penalties resulting from the operation of the ship." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Recital 63 and Article 23(8), European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

Industry clause development has occurred. FACT: BIMCO has published a FuelEU Maritime Clause for Time Charter Parties 2024, providing a standardised contractual mechanism for allocating FuelEU penalty exposure between shipowners and charterers. *Source: BIMCO FuelEU Maritime Clause for Time Charter Parties 2024, BIMCO, 2024. https://www.bimco.org/contractual-affairs/bimco-clauses/current-clauses/fueleu-maritime-clause-for-time-charter-parties-2024/ [Listed under New Sources Identified]

**Action:** Legal counsel to review all ocean carrier contracts and master service agreements to confirm whether FuelEU penalty pass-through language exists, and whether the workspace has inadvertently accepted liability through any charterer-like agreement structure.

---

## MONITORING — Norway and Iceland Port Routing Decisions

*Operational implication:* For trade lanes using Norwegian or Icelandic ports as interim calls, those ports currently function as third-country ports (50% scope rather than 100%) under FuelEU, because EEA incorporation has been delayed. This creates a temporary routing cost differential that will close once incorporation is completed. Workspaces with cargo moving through these corridors should monitor EEA incorporation progress.

**Re-check window:** Incorporation timeline not publicly fixed as of the date of this brief. Monitor quarterly via the Norwegian Maritime Authority and EC DG MOVE announcements.

---

## 10.1 Flexibility Mechanisms — Banking, Borrowing, Pooling

FACT: "On the basis of the calculations undertaken in accordance with Article 16(4), where the ship has, for the reporting period, a compliance surplus ..., the company may bank it to the same ship's compliance balance for the following reporting period." Banking must be recorded in the FuelEU database before the FuelEU document of compliance has been issued; it cannot be banked thereafter. There is no expiry on banked surplus; it follows the ship on change of company. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Article 20(1), European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

FACT: "On the basis of the calculations undertaken in accordance with Article 16(4), where the ship has, for the reporting period, a compliance deficit, the company may borrow an advance compliance surplus of the corresponding amount from the following reporting period. The advance compliance surplus shall be added to the ship's compliance balance in the reporting period and the advance compliance surplus multiplied by 1,1 shall be subtracted from the same ship's compliance balance in the following reporting period." Borrowing is limited: "(a) for an amount exceeding by more than 2% the limit set out in Article 4(2), multiplied by the energy consumption of the ship ...; (b) for two consecutive reporting periods." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Article 20(2), European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

FACT: "The compliance balances for GHG intensity ... of two or more ships ... may be pooled ... A ship's compliance balance may not be included in more than one pool in the same reporting period." The total pooled compliance must be positive; ships entering with a deficit cannot have a higher deficit after allocation; ships entering with a surplus cannot have a deficit after allocation. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Article 21(1) and (4), European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

**Qualification — cross-company pooling.** FACT: "Where the ships participating in the pool are controlled by two or more companies, the pool details registered in the FuelEU database ... shall be validated in the FuelEU database by all the companies concerned in the pool." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Article 21(3), European Parliament and Council, 13 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

*Analytical inference:* The pooling mechanism is the most commercially relevant flexibility tool for workspaces whose carrier networks include both compliant (modern, low-carbon fuel) and non-compliant (older, fossil fuel) vessels. A carrier operating a mixed fleet can pool surplus from newer vessels to offset older vessel deficits, reducing net penalty exposure and thereby reducing pass-through cost pressure on the workspace.

## 10.2 Contractual Liability Allocation — Charter Party Clauses

FACT: BIMCO has published a FuelEU Maritime Clause for Time Charter Parties 2024. This clause creates a framework for allocating FuelEU compliance costs and penalty exposure between shipowners and time charterers who control fuel purchase decisions. *Source: BIMCO FuelEU Maritime Clause for Time Charter Parties 2024, BIMCO, 2024. https://www.bimco.org/contractual-affairs/bimco-clauses/current-clauses/fueleu-maritime-clause-for-time-charter-parties-2024/ [New Sources Identified]

*Industry interpretation:* The BIMCO clause has been cited by multiple maritime law practitioners as the emerging market standard for time charters. Workspaces entering into any charter-like arrangement with ocean carriers should request confirmation of which FuelEU allocation clause governs the relationship.

## 10.3 Carrier Compliance Monitoring Infrastructure (Gap for Workspace)

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces procuring ocean freight as intermediaries currently lack standardised tools to verify:
- Whether a vessel serving a given shipment holds a valid FuelEU DoC.
- Whether the carrier's compliance balance will generate pass-through costs in 2026.
- Whether the carrier's pooling or banking strategy affects service continuity.

These gaps are operationally analogous to the carrier-verification gaps that emerged when EU ETS maritime was introduced. The workspace should add FuelEU DoC status to its carrier qualification checklist.

---
